focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Jin had not slept. He sat with his back against the west wall of the main hall, Melie curled against his thigh, her breathing damp on his forearm. The stone held the night's cold. Around them, in the dimness before the oil lamps were relit, the compound was a room of people who had given up pretending to rest — knees drawn up, blankets twisted, the particular stillness of bodies that had stopped trying to sleep and were instead waiting for morning to give them permission to move.

The previous night's rupture was still in the room. Not metaphorically — it was in the way people had arranged themselves on the floor. The Kondos were pulled tight around their sleeping mats in a knot that excluded Arash, who slept or did not sleep near the south wall with his back to his father. Bett Dao sat upright against the far column with her legs stretched out in front of her, Hala's hand on her knee, though both of them were awake. The Okoyes were a cluster around baby Ada. Suna Mera had her three children arranged close to the kitchen doorway. The Leos kept to their corner, Vera's arm around Pik.

Jin watched them all. He had become a watcher over these eighteen days, and watching was the only thing he was good at here. Good at watching, bad at everything else — at talking, at declaring, at holding his own story together in front of the whole hall after they had heard it crack.

Melie stirred. Her hand found the hem of his shirt and held it. She did this in her sleep now, as though she needed proof he was still there. He didn't move.

When the custodians' bell sounded — one strike, flat and dull — no one seemed surprised. It was too early for the naming. It didn't matter. Fasa appeared in the doorway from the covered walkway, her hair wrapped in cloth, her face carrying...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e me if I don't take that on faith," Vera said. "Given what the naming has done to the people in this compound over the past two weeks."

Fasa had no answer for that. The naming had cracked Jin's story open. It had forced Mava's grief into the room. It had made Bett name a dead child she had carried in silence for seven years. The naming's record was not gentle, and Fasa knew it.

"I understand," Fasa said. And she did. "Tell Cade. Talk to Wynn. I won't raise it in the naming until you're ready."

Vera picked up her mending. Her hands were not steady, but she threaded the needle through the torn seam and pulled, and the motion was the same deliberate motion Fasa had watched her make every morning since the families arrived — the hands of a woman who knew that fabric tears and can be repaired, that the mending is never finished, that you keep stitching because the alternative is to let everything come apart.

"One more thing," Vera said, not looking up from the needle. "The woman — Gitta Tesh. Does she know?"

"Not yet."

"Don't tell her. Not before I've talked to my family." She pulled the thread taut and snapped it with her teeth. "We get to be frightened first. She can wait."

Fasa nodded. She left Vera to her mending and stopped halfway across the courtyard. Cade Leo was at the main hall doorway laying out bowls for breakfast, glancing up every few breaths to locate Pik. Wynn was behind him folding blankets. Neither of them knew yet. Vera had bought them a little time with one hard sentence. It wasn't much.

The bell would ring soon. When it did, Fasa would have to ask the Leos to stand up in front of everyone and answer for a child whose origins they could not name and whose body had begun answering anyway. Below the hill, the flood kept grinding at the buried road.
